text:
Border deaths—the premature loss of life among individuals whose movement has been irregularized—occupy a paradoxical space in contemporary discourse. In the media, these deaths are frequently weaponized to manufacture "crises" or to naturalize fatality as an inevitable byproduct of illegalized migration. Conversely, in official state statistics, these individuals remain ghosts; because authorities rarely record migration status during post-mortem procedures, deceased refugees are invisibly folded into general mortality data. This systemic erasure has forced local actors, ranging from cemetery workers to activists, to step in. Yet, documentation motives vary, and state-level interest only surfaced following heightened public scrutiny.

This article traces the evolution of these recording efforts, contrasting the pioneering work of the UNITED for Intercultural Action network with the more recent Missing Migrants Project by the International Organization for Migration (IOM). Since the 1990s, UNITED has politicized these deaths, framing them as "death by policy" to expose structural state violence. In contrast, the IOM’s project—emerging after the 2013 Lampedusa tragedy—has been critiqued for "humanitarianizing" and naturalizing these losses. By focusing on transit and smuggling, the IOM approach often obscures state responsibility, aligning instead with a growing migration industry that blends care with control.

The paper further explores the "game-changing" potential of data-driven advocacy. In contexts like Croatia, where wartime casualties remain a volatile political issue, border death tolls curiously encounter little resistance. Instead, they circulate as an "amorphous mass" of contradictory figures that fail to trigger meaningful policy shifts. While even a marginal increase in asylum applications can prompt the reintroduction of Schengen border controls, the steady rise in fatalities produces no such urgency.

Drawing on feminist, decolonial, and abolitionist critiques, the article asks: serves documentation to challenge the border regime, or does it merely reinforce the "spectacle of violence"? By reducing victims to data points, do researchers and activists inadvertently solidify their own positions of power? I argue that the appropriation of activist practices by intergovernmental bodies like the IOM may actually sustain systems of dehumanization. However, the article concludes by looking toward grassroots and "vernacular" documentation efforts by survivors and families. Rooted in grief, resentment, and what Clare Hemmings calls "affective solidarity," these practices offer a transformative potential that transcends mere statistics, fostering a desire for structural change born from shared discomfort and the refusal to let these lives remain ungrievable.
